name: mamushi
documentation:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25791237/198873887-f01f9f69-5a1d-4a5f-95cf-1f1d6dfb63fb.png)


# Mamushi
[![image](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/mamushi.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/mamushi/)
[![Build Status](https://github.com/benber86/mamushi/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/benber86/mamushi/actions)
[![codecov](https://codecov.io/github/benber86/mamushi/branch/main/graph/badge.svg?token=WF0YO4ACIT)](https://codecov.io/github/benber86/mamushi)
[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)



Mamushi is a fork of the popular [Black](https://github.com/psf/black) formatter adapted to the [Vyper](https://github.com/vyperlang/vyper/) programming language. Mamushi reformats your Vyper contracts in a readable and consistent way.


## Installation

`pip install mamushi`

## Usage

Search all *.vy files and overwrite them after formatting:

`mamushi`

Specify a list of *.vy files or directories and output to console after formatting:

`mamushi [SRC]`

Output the result to console instead of overwriting:


`mamushi --in-place False`


## Notes

#### Line length

The default line length is 80. Line length can be adjusted by using the `--line-length` option.

#### AST Safety
By default, mamushi will compare the AST of your reformatted code with that of the original to ensure that the changes applied remain strictly formal. The option can be disabled with `--safe False` to speed things up.


#### Trailing commas

When handling expressions split by commas, mamushi follows Black's [default behavior](https://test-black.readthedocs.io/en/style-guide/style_guide/trailing_commas.html).

Mamushi also uses Black's [magic trailing comma](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/the_black_code_style/current_style.html#pragmatism) to give user the option to collapse a comma-separated expression into one line if possible. If a trailing comma is added, mamushi will always explode the expression. This can have important consequences for the commenting of your code. Consider the following two examples:

This code snippet:

```
self.b(0, # amount to send
       msg.sender, # sender
       True, # refund ?
        )
```

formats to the following with a trailing comma after the last argument (`True`):

```
self.b(
    0,  # amount to send
    msg.sender,  # sender
    True,  # refund ?
)
```

but if the trailing comma is removed, the line will be collapsed to:

```
self.b(0, msg.sender, True)  # amount to send  # sender  # refund ?
```

## Future developments

- [ ] Multiprocessing when processing multiple files
- [ ] Configuration files
- [ ] Improve Windows compatibility
- [ ] Handle versioning of Vyper/lark grammar
- [ ] Refactoring comment handling in the parser
- [ ] Add .gitignore / exclude / include support

modules.name: mamushi.formatting.strings
documentation: Simple formatting on strings. Further string formatting code is in trans.py.

documentation:
Returns:
    True iff @string starts with three quotation characters.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.strings.is_multiline_string
documentation: Return True if `leaf` is a multiline string that actually spans many lines.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.strings.sub_twice
documentation:
Replace `regex` with `replacement` twice on `original`.

This is used by string normalization to perform replaces on
overlapping matches.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.strings.lines_with_leading_tabs_expanded
documentation:
Splits string into lines and expands only leading tabs (following the normal
Python rules)

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.strings._cached_compile
documentation: Re(gex) does actually cache patterns internally but this still improves
performance on a long list literal of strings by 5-9% since lru_cache's
caching overhead is much lower.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.strings.normalize_string_quotes
documentation:
Prefer double quotes but only if it doesn't cause more escaping.

Adds or removes backslashes as appropriate. Doesn't parse and fix
strings nested in f-strings.

modules.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets
documentation: Builds on top of nodes.py to track brackets.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.is_split_after_delimiter
documentation:
Return the priority of the `leaf` delimiter, given a line break after it.

The delimiter priorities returned here are from those delimiters that would
cause a line break after themselves.

Higher numbers are higher priority.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.is_split_before_delimiter
documentation:
Return the priority of the `leaf` delimiter, given a line break before it.

The delimiter priorities returned here are from those delimiters that would
cause a line break before themselves.

Higher numbers are higher priority.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketMatchError
documentation: Raised when an opening bracket is unable to be matched to a closing bracket.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker.mark
documentation:
Mark `leaf` with bracket-related metadata. Keep track of delimiters.

All leaves receive an int `bracket_depth` field that stores how deep
within brackets a given leaf is. 0 means there are no enclosing brackets
that started on this line.

If a leaf is itself a closing bracket, it receives an `opening_bracket`
field that it forms a pair with. This is a one-directional link to
avoid reference cycles.

If a leaf is a delimiter (a token on which Black can split the line if
needed) and it's on depth 0, its `id()` is stored in the tracker's
`delimiters` field.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker.any_open_brackets
documentation: Return True if there is an yet unmatched open bracket on the line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker.max_delimiter_priority
documentation:
Return the highest priority of a delimiter found on the line.

Values are consistent with what `is_split_*_delimiter()` return.
Raises ValueError on no delimiters.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker.delimiter_count_with_priority
documentation:
Return the number of delimiters with the given `priority`.

If no `priority` is passed, defaults to max priority on the line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker.maybe_increment_for_loop_variable
documentation:
In a for loop, or comprehension, the variables are often unpacks.

To avoid splitting on the comma in this situation, increase the depth of
tokens between `for` and `in`.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.brackets.BracketTracker.maybe_decrement_after_for_loop_variable
documentation: See `maybe_increment_for_loop_variable` above for explanation.
Keeps track of brackets on a line.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.comments.add_leading_space_after_hashtag
documentation: Add a space between hashtag and first character if none are there

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.comments.settle_prefix
documentation: Puts the leading line returns in prefix

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.nodes.wrap_in_parentheses
documentation:
Wrap `child` in parentheses.

This replaces `child` with an atom holding the parentheses and the old
child.  That requires moving the prefix.

If `visible` is False, the leaves will be valueless (and thus invisible).

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.nodes.is_atom_with_invisible_parens
documentation:
Given a `LN`, determines whether it's an atom `node` with invisible
parens. Useful in dedupe-ing and normalizing parens.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.nodes.ensure_visible
documentation:
Make sure parentheses are visible.

They could be invisible as part of some statements (see
{func}`normalize_invisible_parens` and {func}`visit_import_from`).

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.nodes.is_one_sequence_between
documentation: Return True if content between `opening` and `closing` is a one-sequence.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.nodes.replace_child
documentation:
Side Effects:
    * If @old_child.parent is set, replace @old_child with @new_child in
    @old_child's underlying Node structure.
        OR
    * Otherwise, this function does nothing.

modules.name: mamushi.utils.report
documentation: Summarize Black runs to users.

classes.name: mamushi.utils.report.NothingChanged
documentation: Raised when reformatted code is the same as source.

classes.name: mamushi.utils.report.Report

documentation: Increment the counter for successful reformatting. Write out a message.

methods.name: mamushi.utils.report.Report.failed
documentation: Increment the counter for failed reformatting. Write out a message.

methods.name: mamushi.utils.report.Report.return_code
mamushi.utils.report.Report.__str__
documentation:
Render a color report of the current state.

Use `click.unstyle` to remove colors.
Provides a reformatting counter. Can be rendered with `str(report)`.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.next_leaf
documentation: Return the first leaf that precedes `node`, if any.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.normalize_invisible_parens
documentation:
Make existing optional parentheses invisible or create new ones.

`parens_after` is a set of string leaf values immediately after which parens
should be put.

Standardizes on visible parentheses for single-element tuples, and keeps
existing visible parentheses for other tuples and generator expressions.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.maybe_make_parens_invisible_in_atom
documentation:
If it's safe, make the parens in the atom `node` invisible, recursively.
Additionally, remove repeated, adjacent invisible parens from the atom `node`
as they are redundant.

Returns whether the node should itself be wrapped in invisible parentheses.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.line
documentation:
Generate a line.
If the line is empty, only emit if it makes sense.
If the line is too long, split it first and then generate.
If any lines were generated, set up a new current_line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit__INDENT
documentation: Increase indentation level, maybe yield a line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit__DEDENT
documentation: Decrease indentation level, maybe yield a line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit_call
documentation:
Handles calls depending on whether they're a standalone statement
or part of a compound statement

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit_import
documentation: Handles different import syntax

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit_stmt
documentation: Visit a statement.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit_simple_stmt
documentation: A statement without nested statements.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.visit_decorators
documentation: Visit decorators.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.LineGenerator.__post_init__
documentation: You are in a twisty little maze of passages.

Generates reformatted Line objects.  Empty lines are not emitted.
Note: destroys the tree it's visiting by mutating prefixes of its leaves
in ways that will no longer stringify to valid Python code on the tree.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.EmptyLineTracker

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.linegen.EmptyLineTracker.maybe_empty_lines
documentation:
Returns the number of extra empty lines before and after the `current_line`.
This is for separating `def`, `async def` and `class` with extra empty lines
(two on module-level), as well as providing an extra empty line after flow
control keywords to make them more prominent.

Provides a stateful method that returns the number of potential extra
empty lines needed before and after the currently processed line.
Note: this tracker works on lines that haven't been split yet.

modules.name: mamushi.utils.output
documentation:
Nice output for Black.

The double calls are for patching purposes in tests.

functions.name: mamushi.utils.output.diff
documentation: Return a unified diff string between strings `a` and `b`.

functions.name: mamushi.utils.output.color_diff
documentation: Inject the ANSI color codes to the diff.

functions.name: mamushi.utils.output.dump_to_file
documentation: Dump `output` to a temporary file. Return path to the file.

functions.name: mamushi.format_stdin_to_stdout
documentation:
Format file on stdin. Return True if changed.

If content is None, it's read from sys.stdin.

If `write_back` is YES, write reformatted code back to stdout. If it is DIFF,
write a diff to stdout. The `mode` argument is passed to
{func}`format_file_contents`.

modules.name: mamushi.parsing.tokens
documentation: Punctuation

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.indenter.Indenter.always_accept
documentation: XXX Hack for ContextualLexer. Maybe there's a more elegant solution?

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.right_hand_split
documentation:
Split line into many lines, starting with the last matching bracket pair.

If the split was by optional parentheses, attempt splitting without them, too.
`omit` is a collection of closing bracket IDs that shouldn't be considered for
this split.

Note: running this function modifies `bracket_depth` on the leaves of `line`.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.bracket_split_build_line
documentation:
Return a new line with given `leaves` and respective comments from `original`.

If `is_body` is True, the result line is one-indented inside brackets and as such
has its first leaf's prefix normalized and a trailing comma added when expected.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.should_split_line
documentation: Should `line` be immediately split with `delimiter_split()` after RHS?

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.line_to_string
documentation:
Returns the string representation of @line.

WARNING: This is known to be computationally expensive.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.is_line_short_enough
documentation:
Return True if `line` is no longer than `line_length`.

Uses the provided `line_str` rendering, if any, otherwise computes a new one.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.can_omit_invisible_parens
documentation:
Does `line` have a shape safe to reformat without optional parens around it?

Returns True for only a subset of potentially nice looking formattings but
the point is to not return false positives that end up producing lines that
are too long.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines._can_omit_opening_paren
documentation: See `can_omit_invisible_parens`.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines._can_omit_closing_paren
documentation: See `can_omit_invisible_parens`.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise
documentation:
Raise {exc}`CannotSplit` if the last left- or right-hand split failed.

Do nothing otherwise.

A left- or right-hand split is based on a pair of brackets. Content before
(and including) the opening bracket is left on one line, content inside the
brackets is put on a separate line, and finally content starting with and
following the closing bracket is put on a separate line.

Those are called `head`, `body`, and `tail`, respectively. If the split
produced the same line (all content in `head`) or ended up with an empty `body`
and the `tail` is just the closing bracket, then it's considered failed.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.is_import
documentation: Returns True if the given leaf starts an import statement.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.enumerate_reversed
documentation: Like `reversed(enumerate(sequence))` if that were possible.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.left_hand_split
documentation:
Split line into many lines, starting with the first matching bracket pair.

Note: this usually looks weird, only use this for function definitions.
Prefer RHS otherwise. This is why this function is not symmetrical with
{func}`right_hand_split` which also handles optional parentheses.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.normalize_prefix
documentation:
Leave existing extra newlines if not `inside_brackets`. Remove everything
else.

Note: don't use backslashes for formatting or you'll lose your voting rights.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.dont_increase_indentation
documentation:
Normalize prefix of the first leaf in every line returned by `split_func`.

This is a decorator over relevant split functions.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.delimiter_split
documentation:
Split according to delimiters of the highest priority.

If the appropriate Features are given, the split will add trailing commas
also in function signatures and calls that contain `*` and `**`.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.split_line
documentation:
Splits a `line` into potentially many lines.
They should fit in the allotted `line_length` but might not be able to.
`inner` signifies that there were a pair of brackets somewhere around the
current `line`, possibly transitively. This means we can fallback to splitting
by delimiters if the LHS/RHS don't yield any results.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.generate_trailers_to_omit
documentation:
Generate sets of closing bracket IDs that should be omitted in a RHS.

Brackets can be omitted if the entire trailer up to and including
a preceding closing bracket fits in one line.

Yielded sets are cumulative (contain results of previous yields, too).  First
set is empty, unless the line should explode, in which case bracket pairs until
the one that needs to explode are omitted.

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.append_leaves
documentation:
Append leaves (taken from @old_line) to @new_line, making sure to fix the
underlying Node structure where appropriate.

All of the leaves in @leaves are duplicated. The duplicates are then
appended to @new_line and used to replace their originals in the underlying
Node structure. Any comments attached to the old leaves are reattached to
the new leaves.

Pre-conditions:
    set(@leaves) is a subset of set(@old_line.leaves).

functions.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.hug_power_op
documentation: A transformer which normalizes spacing around power operators.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.CannotTransform
documentation: Base class for errors raised by Transformers.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.CannotSplit
documentation: A readable split that fits the allotted line length is impossible.

classes.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.append
documentation:
Add a new `leaf` to the end of the line.

Unless `preformatted` is True, the `leaf` will receive a new consistent
whitespace prefix and metadata applied by {class}`BracketTracker`.
Trailing commas are maybe removed, unpacked for loop variables are
demoted from being delimiters.

Inline comments are put aside.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.append_safe
documentation:
Like {func}`append()` but disallow invalid standalone comment structure.

Raises ValueError when any `leaf` is appended after a standalone comment
or when a standalone comment is not the first leaf on the line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.is_comment
documentation: Is this line a standalone comment?

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.is_decorator
documentation: Is this line a decorator?

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.is_import
documentation: Is this an import line?

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.contains_standalone_comments
documentation: If so, needs to be split before emitting.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.has_magic_trailing_comma
documentation:
Return True if we have a magic trailing comma, that is when:
- there's a trailing comma here
- it's not a one-tuple
- it's not a single-element subscript
Additionally, if ensure_removable:
- it's not from square bracket indexing
(specifically, single-element square bracket indexing with
Preview.skip_magic_trailing_comma_in_subscript)

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.append_comment
documentation: Add an inline or standalone comment to the line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.comments_after
documentation: Generate comments that should appear directly after `leaf`.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.enumerate_with_length
documentation:
Return an enumeration of leaves with their length.

Stops prematurely on standalone comments.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.__str__
documentation: Render the line.

methods.name: mamushi.formatting.lines.Line.__bool__
documentation: Return True if the line has leaves or comments.
Holds leaves and comments. Can be printed with `str(line)`.

modules.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree
documentation:
Python parse tree definitions.

This is a very concrete parse tree; we need to keep every token and
even the comments and whitespace between tokens.

There's also a pattern matching implementation here.
Copyright 2006 Google, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Licensed to PSF under a Contributor Agreement.

variables.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.HUGE
documentation: maximum repeat count, default max

classes.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.__new__
documentation: Constructor that prevents Base from being instantiated.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.__eq__
documentation:
Compare two nodes for equality.

This calls the method _eq().

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base._eq
documentation:
Compare two nodes for equality.

This is called by __eq__ and __ne__.  It is only called if the two nodes
have the same type.  This must be implemented by the concrete subclass.
Nodes should be considered equal if they have the same structure,
ignoring the prefix string and other context information.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.clone
documentation:
Return a cloned (deep) copy of self.

This must be implemented by the concrete subclass.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.post_order
documentation:
Return a post-order iterator for the tree.

This must be implemented by the concrete subclass.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.pre_order
documentation:
Return a pre-order iterator for the tree.

This must be implemented by the concrete subclass.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.replace
documentation: Replace this node with a new one in the parent.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.get_lineno
documentation: Return the line number which generated the invocant node.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.remove
documentation:
Remove the node from the tree. Returns the position of the node in its
parent's children before it was removed.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.next_sibling
documentation:
The node immediately following the invocant in their parent's children
list. If the invocant does not have a next sibling, it is None

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.prev_sibling
documentation:
The node immediately preceding the invocant in their parent's children
list. If the invocant does not have a previous sibling, it is None.

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.get_suffix
documentation:
Return the string immediately following the invocant node. This is
effectively equivalent to node.next_sibling.prefix

class_variables.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.parent

class_variables.name: mamushi.parsing.pytree.Base.children
documentation: List of subnodes

Abstract base class for Node and Leaf.

This provides some default functionality and boilerplate using the
template pattern.

A node may be a subnode of at most one parent.

functions.name: mamushi.parsing.comparator.compare_ast
documentation:
Generates two ASTs with no punctuation tokens  and uses a transformer to
remove docstrings (since these are reformatted and not ignored)
and newlines (since we might reformat comments) then compares
the two trees to ensure no syntactic changes were introduced

modules.name: mamushi.parsing.parser
documentation: EXPERIMENTAL VYPER PARSER
https://github.com/vyperlang/vyper/

classes.name: mamushi.parsing.parser.Parser

methods.name: mamushi.parsing.parser.Parser._generate_comment_associations
documentation:
Run a BFS on the tree to find the final leaves of each lines
where we can append the ignored comments in the final tree
return a mapping of leaves to comment

Parse a vyper file using lark, converts the lark tree to a pytree
Lalr needs comments that aren't parsed along with newlines to be ignored
We collect those with callbacks and then reintegrate them during the
conversion to pytree
